text: An atomically manganese (Mn) incorporated aryl amino-substituted graphitic carbon nitride (g-C 3 N 4 ) (AN-Mn/C 3 N 4 ) catalyst is prepared by hydrothermal method and used to generate hydrogen peroxide (H 2 O 2 ) in situ from seawater. The prepared catalyst shows excellent catalytic performance, achieving up to 2230 μM H 2 O 2 in 7 h using alkaline water and 1800 μM H 2 O 2 in seawater under the same conditions. The as-prepared AN-Mn/C 3 N 4 catalyst shows good stability and can be quickly recovered and reused for several cycles without obvious decrease in the catalytic activity. In situ operando synchrotron absorption spectroscopy, X-ray scattering technique and solid-state NMR show that the AN-Mn/C 3 N 4 catalyst conducts a two-electron water oxidation reaction (WOR) to generate hydrogen peroxide, involving both direct and indirect WOR pathways. Photoinduced holes (h + ) in the catalyst can either oxidize water (H 2 O) to hydrogen peroxide (H 2 O 2 ) via a single two-electron process or act on hydroxide (OH - ) to produce hydroxyl radicals (•OH), which react with each other to yield hydrogen peroxide. The atomic configuration of the active sites on AN-Mn/C 3 N 4 catalyst is characterized by a full set of structural analysis methods, including EPR, XANES, EXAFS, HR-TEM, XPS and MAS solid-state NMR. By comparing all the structural information, multiscale molecular modeling involving classical reactive molecular dynamics simulations and quantum calculations has been conducted to reveal the unique activity and selectivity of Mn active sites.
